---
title: "Mapping & Rules"
---

## How LinkML constructs land in data-dict

The generator reads the schema with `SchemaView` and emits the relational part
of it. Here's what maps to what:

| LinkML | data-dict.yaml | Notes |
|--------|----------------|-------|
| `schema.name` / `title` / `description` | top-level `name` / `label` / `description` | straight across |
| `schema.version` | `version.number` | |
| a concrete `class` | a `tables[]` entry | one class, one table |
| an `abstract` / `mixin` class | nothing | its slots get flattened into descendants |
| a `tree_root` class | nothing | it's a document wrapper, not a table |
| a `slot` | a `columns[]` entry | |
| `slot.range` | column `type` | see the type table below |
| `slot.identifier: true` | `primary_key` + `number(id)` | also hoisted to the top of the table |
| `slot.required: true` | `required` constraint | |
| `slot.unit.ucum_code` | column `units` (and `number(quantity)`) | |
| enum `permissible_values` | column `values` | as a `{code: label}` map when there are labels |
| `minimum_value` / `maximum_value` | `range: [min, max]` | range types only |
| slot `examples:` | column `examples: [...]` | coerced to the column type; `slot_usage` gives per-table values |
| a slot whose range is a class | `foreign_key` column + a `relationships[]` entry | the relational/object seam |

### Types

| LinkML range | data-dict `type` |
|--------------|------------------|
| `integer` / `float` / `double` / `decimal` | `number`, or `number(quantity)` with a unit, or `number(id)` for an identifier or FK |
| `string` | `string` |
| `boolean` | `boolean` |
| `date` | `date` |
| `datetime` | `datetime` |
| an enum | `enum` |
| a class reference | `number(id)` + `foreign_key` |

## The data-dict type rules

data-dict wants exactly one "representation" on each column, and which one
depends on the type. I couldn't find this written down as a spec, so I read it
out of the validator source,
[`validate_spec.rs`](https://github.com/tidyverse/data-dict/blob/main/crates/data-dict/src/validate_spec.rs)
(the `RANGE_TYPES` list and the match arm that follows). It comes down to:

| Column type | What it must carry | Can LinkML supply it without data? |
|-------------|--------------------|-------------------------------------|
| `enum` | `values` | Yes, from `permissible_values` |
| `boolean` | none of values/range/examples | Yes, trivially |
| `number(ordinal)`, `number(quantity)`, `date`, `datetime` | `range: [min, max]` | Yes, if both bounds are declared |
| `string`, `number`, `number(id)` | a non-empty `examples` list | Yes, from slot `examples:` (otherwise you get a flagged hole) |

Two things the validator corrected for me early:

- `range` is a two-item array, `[min, max]`, not a `{min, max}` object. That was
  my first bug.
- An empty `examples: []` is not allowed, the list has to have something in it.
  And `boolean` columns aren't supposed to have any of the three at all.

## What doesn't make it across

The generator logs everything it drops. Two of those drops are permanent,
because data-dict has no concept to hold them:

- URIs, prefixes, and ontology mappings. There's no semantic layer in data-dict,
  so LinkML's `class_uri`, `slot_uri`, and `mappings` just go away.
- Inheritance. data-dict is flat. An `abstract` or `mixin` class produces no
  table; its slots are copied down into each concrete class that used them
  (`class_induced_slots()`), and the fact that they shared a parent is lost.

Everything else, types, constraints, enums, units, bounds, examples, and the
relationships, comes through intact.
